mission.title: Install and Configure Bfe on Kubernetes
mission.description: BFE (Beyond Front End) is a modern layer 7 load balancer developed by Baidu, designed to manage and route traffic efficiently. Installing BFE allows you to leverage its capabilities for advanced traffic management and load balancing in your Kubernetes environment.

steps.title: Add the BFE Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official BFE Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration. This will allow you to install BFE using Helm.
```bash
helm repo add bfe https://bfenetworks.github.io/bfe-helm-chart
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install BFE using Helm
steps.description:
Install BFE with a specific version in a new namespace called 'bfe'. This command will deploy the BFE server and its components.
```bash
helm install bfe bfe/bfe --version 1.8.1 --namespace bfe --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check the status of the BFE pods to ensure they are running correctly.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace bfe
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Edit the BFE deployment to set resource limits for better performance and stability.
```bash
kubectl edit deployment bfe --namespace bfe
# Add the following under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     cpu: "500m"
#     memory: "512Mi"
#   requests:
#     cpu: "250m"
#     memory: "256Mi"
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for access
steps.description:
Forward a local port to the BFE service to access it from your local machine.
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/bfe --namespace bfe 8080:80
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation will have the BFE pods running in the 'bfe' namespace, and you will be able to access the BFE service through port 8080 on your local machine. You should also see the configured resource limits applied to the BFE deployment.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add bfe https://bfenetworks.github.io/bfe-helm-chart
helm repo update

kubectl edit deployment bfe --namespace bfe
# Add the following under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     cpu: "500m"
#     memory: "512Mi"
#   requests:
#     cpu: "250m"
#     memory: "256Mi"

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
